title: 48" Open-Shelf Bathroom Vanity
summary: A 48" plywood vanity with doors flanking an open center shelf and two drawers. Real casework — carcass, face frame, doors, and inset drawers.
description:
This is a full piece of cabinetry, and it's worth being honest about that before you start: a plywood carcass, a separate face frame, overlay doors, and two inset drawers on euro slides. None of the individual steps are hard, but there are a lot of them and they stack, so accuracy early pays off through the whole build.

The layout is a cabinet with doors on each side and an open shelf in the middle — handy in a bathroom for towels or baskets, and it also gives the plumber a clear run at the trap. Because it's going in a wet room, plan for the sink cutout and the plumbing before you fix the middle shelf; the design keeps that shelf adjustable and cut down for exactly that reason.

The drawers are built to your real openings, not to a drawing. That's the standard casework discipline: build the box, mount the slides, then make the door and drawer faces last to fit the actual gaps. Inset the drawer boxes 3/4" so the faces sit where you want them. A countertop and sink are separate purchases sized to the finished 48" base.

tools.note: The carcass, face frame, and drawer boxes are all pocket-screwed.
For breaking down the plywood sheet into the 20-1/4" strips. A track saw or table saw gives cleaner, straighter rips if you have one.
Square crosscuts on the face-frame and solid stock.
Face frame and drawer bottoms are glued and pinned.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood
materials.note: Carcass. Ripped into 20-1/4" wide strips. Birch or maple ply finishes better than sheathing ply.

materials.name: 1/2" plywood
materials.note: A quarter sheet for the door center panels; you'll use a fraction of it.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood
materials.note: A quarter sheet for the drawer bottoms.

materials.name: 1/2" x 3-1/2" boards
materials.note: Drawer box sides. Any 1/2" stock ripped to 3-1/2".

materials.name: Wood filler and finish
materials.note: Use a moisture-tolerant finish — this lives in a bathroom.

cutList.note: 1/2"-thick door frame members; cut from 1x3 ripped down if needed.

steps.title: Build the carcass box
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" sheet into 20-1/4" wide strips and build the basic box for the vanity with pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws. This box is the foundation — check it square across the diagonals and make sure it sits flat, because every part after this references it.

steps.title: Install the side shelves
steps.body:
Attach the fixed shelves in the two door bays. If you'd rather have adjustable shelves, drill shelf-pin holes instead.

Tip: if you plan to edge-band the front edges, cut this shelf 1/8" shy at the front so the banding sits flush.

steps.title: Set up the center bay for plumbing
steps.body: Build the open center section and make the middle shelf adjustable, cutting it down in size. You'll need to notch or cut its center out to clear the drain and supply lines — do that fitting against the actual rough-in, not a guess. Keeping this shelf removable is what lets a plumber work in there later.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body:
Build the face frame as a separate assembly with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws, flat on the bench where you can keep it square. Then attach the whole frame to the front of the carcass with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue.

A face frame built off the cabinet and dropped on as a unit comes out far cleaner than trying to attach stiles and rails one at a time in place.

steps.title: Add back supports and slide cleats
steps.body: Add the back supports using pocket holes on the back side so they don't show. Then install the cleats the drawer slides mount to, attaching them with nails and glue. The cleats have to be dead level and parallel or the drawers will bind.

steps.title: Build the drawers to your openings
steps.body:
Measure your real openings and your slides, then build the drawer boxes to suit — the pictured boxes are 1/2" stock joined with 1/2" pocket holes and 1" pocket screws, with a 1/4" plywood bottom glued and pinned on.

Install the boxes inset 3/4" from the face frame, which leaves room for the drawer faces to sit flush later.

steps.title: Make the doors and drawer faces to fit
steps.body:
Build the two frame-and-panel doors from the 1x3 frames and 1/2" plywood center panels, and cut the drawer faces, all sized to your actual openings — not the drawing. Hang the doors on the non-mortise hinges and float the drawer faces on the boxes for even reveals.

Making the faces last, to the real gaps, is what gives cabinetry its tight, consistent lines.

steps.title: Sand, finish, and mount hardware
steps.body:
Sand everything, fill any holes, and finish with a moisture-tolerant paint or topcoat — this is bathroom furniture and will meet splashes and humidity daily. Add the knobs and pulls last.

The countertop and sink are separate; buy or cut them to the finished 48" base and seal around the basin.

images.alt: A 48-inch bathroom vanity with cabinet doors on each side and an open shelf in the center
